free_text: Fission fragment mass and energy distributions as a
function of incident neutron energy measured in a
lead slowing-down spectrometer
Main reference
Preliminary data
The incident neutron spectrum is characterized by the
following formula:
Double sided fission chamber with Frisch grid
placed in the lead slowing down spectrometer (LSDS).
LSDS consisted of cube of lead with mass of 75 ton
and side of 1.8 m. Lead was covered with a 0.75-mm
layer of cadmium to prevent neutrons that have escaped
and thermalized from re-entering the lead.
Approved by author C.R. by mail 12. 3. 2010
Compiled by S.H.
Added Method code, modified reaction
in subentry 4
SD: FACILITY, SAMPLE and INC-SOURCE moved
to Subents 2-7.
Minor typogr.corr. in sub.1.
Experiment performed at RPI 60-MeV
electron Linac.
The 235U sample was made at Los Alamos National Labs
and was prepared by dissolving 99.98% 235U in 8M HNO3.
The solution was dried down and redissolved in absolute
alcohol and 25.3+-0.5 mu-g was deposited in an area of
1 cm diameter on a polyimide film. The sample was
placed on cathode in the middle of the fission chamber
Neutrons were produced through a (gamma,n)
reaction when electrons from the RPI 60-MeV electron
linac interact with a tantalum target, placed in the
center of lead slowing down spectrometer.The linac was
run at an average electron current of 8 mu-A,
delivering 58-MeV electrons in a pulse width of 200 ns
at 180 Hz.The neutron energy and flux are experimentaly
known functions of slowing down time and electron beam
intensity. The  neutron-energy resolution (FWHM)] in
the energy range of 0.1 eV to 1 keV was
approximately 35%.
Data presented in fig. 5 of the PR/C,81,014607
reference sent by author (C.R.)
Statistical uncertainty

SD: SF8=MXW -> SPA
Data times normalisation factor 2

resonance group is primarily 225-275 eV.
